rationale_for_baseline_tier: Isolated acute urticaria with a fully negative anaphylaxis screen (no airway/CVS/GI involvement) is a low-acuity, self-limiting condition manageable at home with antihistamines and a routine GP follow-up if needed (T1). The dramatic onset does not justify higher tiering, BUT a hard, explicit anaphylaxis safety-net to T5 is mandatory because urticaria can be the prodrome of anaphylaxis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling of lips/tongue/throat, voice change, difficulty breathing or swallowing, wheeze, dizziness, faintness, or vomiting/abdominal pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately and lie flat; use an adrenaline auto-injector if available
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate progression to anaphylaxis/angioedema — an immediately life-threatening airway/cardiovascular emergency needing adrenaline within minutes.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips, tongue or throat start to swell, your voice changes, you have any trouble breathing or swallowing, or you feel dizzy or faint — call 000 straight away and lie flat. Don't wait to see if it settles.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Wheals persisting beyond 6 weeks or frequently recurring
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests chronic urticaria warranting GP assessment and possible referral; not urgent.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise the anaphylaxis safety-net clearly and advise keeping a phone to hand, since no one else is present to observe deterioration.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can manage this at home with regular non-drowsy antihistamines and cool compresses. The single most important thing: if you get any lip/tongue/throat swelling, voice change, trouble breathing or swallowing, or feel faint — call 000 immediately and lie flat.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with telehealth/GP follow-up; 000 immediately for any anaphylaxis features
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24/7 for any airway or systemic features; otherwise routine antihistamine management overnight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute spontaneous urticaria, likely post-viral, anaphylaxis screen negative; managed with up-dosed non-sedating antihistamine ± short steroid course.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring the patient without giving an explicit anaphylaxis safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Urticaria progresses to airway angioedema overnight; patient does not recognise it as an emergency and delays calling 000.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Not screening airway/CVS/GI before reassurance, missing early anaphylaxis features already present.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Directing an isolated urticaria with a negative anaphylaxis screen to ED/ambulance — unnecessary emergency resource use and patient alarm.
